answer:
\boxed{T.S. Eliot}

The passage describes T.S. Eliot’s scathing critique of W.H. Auden’s poetic settings in his 1941 lectures *The Use of Poetry and the Use of Criticism*, where Eliot famously mocked Auden’s imagery as grotesque and uninspired. Eliot’s opening analogy—imagining a man finding a box of Auden’s works—serves as a satirical introduction to his broader disdain for Auden’s style. This critique remains one of Eliot’s most infamous attacks on a contemporary poet.
